files.title: Questionnaire Definition Source
files.description: Stacked multi-language questionnaire rows with English structure and embedded ASC scale mapping.
files.whatItShows: This is the questionnaire master file before the app loads anything into memory. Each row represents one item in one language, so repeated item ids across languages are expected.
files.whyItMatters: If wording, item order, ASC factor mapping, or induction and dose option sets are wrong, the rest of the workflow inherits that mistake from here.
files.lookFor: Start with language, item_id, label, type, order, scale_id, and the induction or dose option columns.

files.title: Runtime State
files.description: Loaded instrument metadata plus full-table and selected-language runtime shapes.
files.whatItShows: This is the in-app runtime snapshot after the questionnaire definition has been loaded and split into reusable runtime objects.
files.whyItMatters: It shows what the survey actually has available at run time, which is the right place to check when the source file looks correct but the live questionnaire behaves differently.
files.lookFor: Check the instrument identifiers, selected language, and the row or column counts for the full and filtered runtime tables.

files.title: responses_df
files.description: Common long in-memory response table before numeric and text/context rows are split.
files.whatItShows: This is the shared in-memory response table assembled right before persistence and scoring logic split it into specialized subsets.
files.whyItMatters: It is the last place where all answers still travel together in one long shape, so it is the easiest place to understand how the submission is being normalized.
files.lookFor: Check submission_id, item_id, type, and value to see how one questionnaire session turns into one row per answered item.

files.title: Numeric Memory Rows
files.description: Numeric subset used for ASC scoring and later durable writes to response_numeric.
files.whatItShows: This is the numeric-only slice taken from the common response table after text and context rows have been peeled away.
files.whyItMatters: Only these rows feed ASC scoring, so a missing or malformed numeric row here will propagate into the factor scores and later feedback screens.
files.lookFor: Check item_id, numeric value, and created_at to confirm which answers are treated as scoreable numeric inputs.

files.title: Text and Context Memory Rows
files.description: Text and context subset including q0 and q1 values used for peer filter context.
files.whatItShows: This is the text and context slice, including the q0 and q1 fields that later determine the peer comparison bucket.
files.whyItMatters: These rows do not affect ASC scoring directly, but they do control what induction and dose labels are stored and how peer plotting context is resolved.
files.lookFor: Check field_id and text, especially the q0 and q1 rows, because those are the values that later become raw and canonical peer context.

files.title: ASC Factor Scores
files.description: Canonical ASC factor score rows keyed by scale_id and used for immediate self-feedback.
files.whatItShows: This is the canonical ASC output created from the numeric response rows, one row per scale_id for the current submission.
files.whyItMatters: These are the values shown back to the participant and later stored durably, so they are the core scoring truth for one session.
files.lookFor: Check scale_id and score_value to confirm each ASC factor was produced once and mapped to the expected canonical scale.

files.title: submission Table
files.description: Submission-level metadata written once per survey session.
files.whatItShows: This is the durable session header table, with one row representing one survey submission.
files.whyItMatters: It anchors the rest of the normalized storage model, because numeric answers, text rows, and factor scores all tie back to submission_id.
files.lookFor: Check submission_id, instrument identifiers, and created_at to see the metadata that scopes the rest of the stored rows.

files.title: response_numeric Table
files.description: Durable numeric answer rows keyed by submission_id and item_id.
files.whatItShows: This is the durable numeric answer table after the in-memory numeric subset has been written to MariaDB.
files.whyItMatters: If the submission exists but scoring or exports look incomplete, this table shows whether the numeric rows actually landed in storage.
files.lookFor: Check submission_id, item_id, and value to confirm that every scoreable answer now exists as its own durable row.

files.title: response_text Table
files.description: Durable text and context rows that are excluded from public export.
files.whatItShows: This is the durable text and context table, storing free-text fields plus context answers such as induction and dose.
files.whyItMatters: It preserves the raw context needed for peer canonicalization and private analysis, while also showing what must stay out of the public export.
files.lookFor: Check field_id and text to distinguish peer-filter context rows like q0 and q1 from other text fields that remain private.

files.title: score Table
files.description: Durable ASC factor score rows with canonical scale_id and score_value.
files.whatItShows: This is the durable ASC score table, storing one canonical factor score row per submission and scale.
files.whyItMatters: Peer plotting and exports both depend on these rows, so they are the durable form of the participant profile shown in feedback.
files.lookFor: Check submission_id, scale_id, and score_value to verify the stored factor profile matches the in-memory scoring result.

files.title: Peer Cache Build
files.description: Shows how one peer_plot_submission_cache row is assembled from submission, response_text context, and score completeness.
files.whatItShows: This is a build recipe for one peer cache row, showing how submission metadata, raw q0 and q1 values, and score completeness are combined.
files.whyItMatters: It makes the peer cache less mysterious by showing exactly which stored inputs become the one-row-per-submission context used later in feedback.
files.lookFor: Check the source fragments, canonicalization result, and completeness flags to see how one submission becomes one peer cache record.

files.title: Peer Selection State
files.description: Shows the canonical control catalog, the current submission defaults, and the selected canonical pair used for the peer query.
files.whatItShows: This is the feedback-step selection state that powers the comparison dropdowns and the active peer query.
files.whyItMatters: It explains why users only see canonical buckets in the controls even when the stored raw labels were translated, free text, or phrased differently.
files.lookFor: Check the control catalog, the current submission defaults, and the selected canonical induction and dose pair.

files.title: peer_plot_submission_cache
files.description: Submission-level peer plotting context with raw and canonical induction and dose values.
files.whatItShows: This is the actual one-row-per-submission peer cache that sits between durable storage and the feedback query.
files.whyItMatters: It shows both the raw context labels and the canonical tokens, which is what lets the app compare submissions consistently across spelling, translation, or free-text variation.
files.lookFor: Check the raw versus canonical induction and dose columns, along with completeness flags, to see which submissions qualify for peer plotting.

files.title: Peer Plot Score Query
files.description: One row per matching peer and ASC factor returned for the chosen canonical bucket.
files.whatItShows: This is the filtered score result set used for plotting once a canonical peer bucket has been selected.
files.whyItMatters: It is the closest raw input to the plot itself, because each row becomes one peer value on one ASC spoke in the final visualization.
files.lookFor: Check peer_id, scale_id, and score_value to see how many peers matched and how many factor rows the renderer receives.

files.title: Peer Plot Render Payload
files.description: Explicit summary of the current feedback render: self profile, peer violin layer, quartile summary, legend note, and share metadata.
files.whatItShows: This is the fully assembled render payload for the feedback plot, including the selected bucket, the participant profile, peer summaries, and share metadata.
files.whyItMatters: It shows what the browser actually receives to draw the violin-style peer plot, the legend, and the shareable snapshot context.
files.lookFor: Check the selected bucket, peer count, self profile, violin and quartile summaries, and the extra legend or share fields that frame the chart.

files.title: Public Wide Export
files.description: Public anonymized wide export with session_anon, item_* columns, and scale_* columns.
files.whatItShows: This is the publishable wide export, with one anonymized row per session and separate columns for item answers and scale scores.
files.whyItMatters: It is the main public data shape collaborators receive when they need a shareable bundle without raw identifiers or free-text content.
files.lookFor: Check session_anon, item_* columns, and scale_* columns to see how normalized tables are widened into one public row per session.

files.title: Public Factor Export
files.description: Public factor-only export with one row per anonymized peer_id and scale_id.
files.whatItShows: This is the long public factor-only export, keeping just anonymized ids plus one row per scale score.
files.whyItMatters: It is the simplest public analysis shape when a collaborator only needs ASC factor scores and does not need the full wide questionnaire table.
files.lookFor: Check peer_id, scale_id, and score_value to see the long-format public score table used for factor-level analysis.

files.title: Private Full Export
files.description: Private full export that keeps submission_id, created_at, text_*, item_*, and scale_* columns.
files.whatItShows: This is the private wide export, keeping the full joined row with raw submission identity, timestamps, text fields, items, and scale scores.
files.whyItMatters: It represents the highest-context export shape used for internal work where the public anonymization and text stripping would be too limiting.
files.lookFor: Check submission_id, created_at, text_* columns, item_* columns, and scale_* columns to understand what is retained only in the private bundle.
